result:
Mutable balance row: one row per card, UPDATE on redeem.
Pros: simplest read (SELECT balance), matches existing wallet pattern, fewer joins, cheaper storage.
Cons: no history — can't answer "why is balance $12?" without separate audit log. Concurrent updates need row lock or CAS. Refunds/reversals mutate in place, lose provenance. Bugs overwrite truth silently.

Event-sourced ledger: append-only rows (issue, redeem, refund, expire, adjust), balance = SUM(amount).
Pros: full audit for free, refunds are new events not mutations, double-entry friendly for finance reconciliation, concurrency via insert-only (no lock contention on balance row), easy to replay or fix bad events with compensating entries, regulators/accountants expect this for money instruments.
Cons: reads need aggregation — mitigate with cached balance column updated in same tx, or materialized view. More schema. Team must resist "just UPDATE the balance" shortcut.

Hybrid common: ledger table + denormalized balance column on gift_card row, both written in one tx. Reads hit the column, audits hit the ledger. Reconciliation job asserts SUM(ledger) == balance.

For this case: gift cards money-like (legally regulated in many jurisdictions, refundable, expire, subject to escheatment). Ledger wins on audit and refund semantics even if wallets are mutable. Wallet precedent not binding — wallets may be internal credit with different legal footing.

Recommend hybrid. Ledger as truth, cached balance for read path.

Missing input: do wallets ever need audit/refund today, or is that pain you've already hit? If yes, consider migrating wallets to same pattern later.
